coredata.dc:title: Tribomechanical promotion of photon emission on the fullerene C60 coated titanium surfaces
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Chengtian
Dorner-Reisel, Annett
Wang, Tao
Freiberger, Emma
Schneider, Doreen
Ritter, Uwe
Moje, Jens
coredata.dc:description: Fullerene C60 films on titanium substrates were tribological loaded by a sliding diamond tip using different normal forces of 0.5 N, 1 N, 2 N, 3 N, 5 N and a sliding velocity of 10 mm/min. The fullerene C60 improved the sliding resistance of the titanium. Two mechanisms were identified: (i) During sliding of the diamond tip against the C60 coated titanium with normal loads of 1 N to 5 N, a more homogeneous friction behavior was observed in comparison to titanium without C60 coating. The fullerene C60 film seems to reduce sticking behavior. (ii) Applying a low normal force of 0.5 N, the sliding seemed to involve the fullerene C60 film only. An excitingly low coefficient of friction of 0.05 ± 0.01 was determined. It seemed that the fullerene C60 molecules effected less sticking behavior. Furthermore, after the tribomechanical loading, light emission from the sliding traces could be stimulated. The tribo-induced effect continued several hours after the sliding impact. Daylight was used for optical excitation. Light emissions from the wear traces of the tribological stimulated fullerene C60 films on titanium were observed at wavelengths of 605/70 nm, 525/50 nm and 460/50 nm. Photon emission did grow in intensity with the increase of normal force. Such tribo-induced signals could be useful as a smart feature for identifying the loading level or state of wear processes and consumption of the C60 film during the tribological impacts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fullerene C60
Tribocharging
Photoelectricity
Fluorescence microscopy
Coefficient of friction
Smart films

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Friction behavior of titanium without and with fullerene C60

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: German Ministry for Research and Technology

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors acknowledge the financial support by German Ministry for Research and Technology (Bundesministerium f\u00FCr Bildung und Forschung, BMBF, grant no. 13GW0401). The authors are thankful for the support by the International Science and Technology Cooperation Programme (2018YFE0194100). Sincere thanks are given to Mr. Hartwig Albrecht (Schmalkalden University of Applied Sciences, Germany) for technical support with the Revetest Scratch Tester RST3 equipment.

The authors acknowledge the financial support by German Ministry for Research and Technology (Bundesministerium f\u00FCr Bildung und Forschung, BMBF, grant no. 13GW0401 ). The authors are thankful for the support by the International Science and Technology Cooperation Programme ( 2018YFE0194100 ). Sincere thanks are given to Ms. Mr. Hartwig Albrecht (Schmalkalden University of Applied Sciences, Germany) for technical support with the Revetest Scratch Tester RST 3 equipment.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Distribution of scrachtes on the sample:
(a) Schematic drawing; (b) polished Ti without C60; (c) polished Ti with fullerene C60 coating.
Coefficients of friction COF of Ti and Ti + C60 (dry, s = 2 mm) applying different normal forces:
Wear volume of Ti and Ti + C₆₀ in laboratory atmosphere (dry; RH ~25 %).
SEM images of (a) uncoated cp-Ti suface; (b) TiC60 surface.

SEM images of sliding traces: (a) Trace after sliding with normal force Fn = 0.5 N; (b) Trace after sliding with normal force of 5.0 N; (c) Inside trace from 0.5 N sliding; (d) Brigth electrical charged particles after longer irradiation with electron beam in (c); (e) Edge of sliding trace Fn = 5.0 N; (f)) Inside trace from 5.0 N sliding.

Detection of light emission on (a,d,g) Titanium without fullerene C60, (b,e,h) Ti + C60 immediately after tribomechanical loading, (c,f,i) Ti + C60 after tribomechanical loading and 1 week time. The light emission was determined with (a,b,c) TRITC filter (light emission wavelength: 605/70 nm), (d,e,f) GFP filter (light emission wavelength: 525/50 nm), (g,h,i) DAPI filter (light emission wavelength: 460/50 nm.
Schematic drawing of the sliding process with a normal force of (a) 0.5 N; (b) 5.0 N.
Photoinduced charge transfer between TiO2 and fullerene.
Fn: normal force; v: sliding velocity; h: Planck number; υ: frequency.
Medical grade samples for tribomechanical tests.
Filter specification of the fluorescence filter set.
Coefficent of friction μ of cp-Ti and Ti + C60 samples.
Wear volume of cp-Ti and Ti + C60 samples after the sliding tests.

head.ce:title: Tribomechanical promotion of photon emission on the fullerene C60 coated titanium surfaces

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: University of Applied Sciences Schmalkalden, Germany
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: University of Applied Sciences Schmalkalden, Germany

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Moje Keramik-Implantate GmbH & Co. KG, Petersberg, Germany
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Moje Keramik-Implantate GmbH & Co. KG, Petersberg, Germany

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fullerene C60 films on titanium substrates were tribological loaded by a sliding diamond tip using different normal forces of 0.5 N, 1 N, 2 N, 3 N, 5 N and a sliding velocity of 10 mm/min. The fullerene C60 improved the sliding resistance of the titanium. Two mechanisms were identified: (i) During sliding of the diamond tip against the C60 coated titanium with normal loads of 1 N to 5 N, a more homogeneous friction behavior was observed in comparison to titanium without C60 coating. The fullerene C60 film seems to reduce sticking behavior. (ii) Applying a low normal force of 0.5 N, the sliding seemed to involve the fullerene C60 film only. An excitingly low coefficient of friction of 0.05 ± 0.01 was determined. It seemed that the fullerene C60 molecules effected less sticking behavior. Furthermore, after the tribomechanical loading, light emission from the sliding traces could be stimulated. The tribo-induced effect continued several hours after the sliding impact. Daylight was used for optical excitation. Light emissions from the wear traces of the tribological stimulated fullerene C60 films on titanium were observed at wavelengths of 605/70 nm, 525/50 nm and 460/50 nm. Photon emission did grow in intensity with the increase of normal force. Such tribo-induced signals could be useful as a smart feature for identifying the loading level or state of wear processes and consumption of the C60 film during the tribological impacts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Although their potential is by far not exploited yet, fullerene C60 films have the capacity to become an important coating material for several purposes, like solar cells, nanomechanical systems or nanorobots [1–3]. Fullerene C60 and its derivatives also have numerous applications in the medical and biological fields [4–6]. Since the discovery of fullerene C60 by Kroto et al. in 1985 [7] and the development of a method for large-scale production of C60 by Krätschmer et al. five years later [8], the unique properties and chemical characteristics of fullerenes have attracted widespread attention in basic science and shown great potential in technological applications. For example, the aqueous dispersion of fullerene C60 has demonstrated high wound healing activity in animal models [9]. Research by Nedzvetsky et al. [10] showed that C60 fullerenes could reduce the production of pro-inflammatory cytokines, demonstrating their anti-inflammatory properties in diabetic complications. A fullerene C60 coating developed by Reynoso et al. [11] was successfully applied in photodynamic inactivation of microorganisms, showing high photostability and excellent antibacterial effects. Fullerene derivatives are being studied for use in drug delivery systems due to their unique electronic and structural properties [12]. In this context, fullerene structures have been presented for nanoactuator purposes by Panwar et al. [13]. Urmi et al. [14] describe that aptamer-nanomaterial hybrids can be engineered for enhanced functionality. They expect a leveraging effect on optical, magnetic, thermal, and electronic characteristics due to aptamer-nanomaterial hybrids. Nanomaterials often serve as quenchers called as nano-quenchers in cases when using FRET (Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer) technology so aptamers frequently employ nanomaterials like quantum dots (QDs) with strong fluorescence characteristics as fluorescent probes. Carbon nanomaterials based sensors in micro- and nanodevices are combining high elasticity, variable bandgap and capacity as such nano-quenchers. Therefore, carbon-based nanomaterials are easily integrated into biosensing and smart device systems. Furthermore, single-strand DNA molecules have capability to adsorb onto carbon nanomaterials due to the hydrophobicity of their surface and π-stacking interaction [15].

As far as we know, excitation of fullerene C60 due to tribomechanical loading by sliding movements on the surface of a fullerene C60 coated photocatalytic agent like oxidised titanium bulk material is novel, especially under low normal forces between 0.5 N and 5.0 N. The selected titanium materials of the present study are used as a biomaterial for dental and other biomedical implants. As reported in own work [16] different processes can occur during tribomechanical loading of fullerene C60. In previous work, we demonstrated that the microstructure and configuration of fullerene C60 molecules is influenced due to the sliding of a zirconia ball. If the zirconia ball slides with 1.0 N normal force and 120 mm/min sliding velocity, single fullerene C60 buckyball monomers formed dimers. Such dimers appear due to a [2 + 2] cycloaddition reaction. Excitation of C60 is able to activate double bonds of neighbouring single C60 molecules for reaction and formation of bindings in a four membered ring arrangement. Furthermore, the atomic coordinates of the 120 atoms of C60-C60 dimers can be adjusted to obtain a configuration with low energy. In this way, the C60-C60 dimers are ordered in a certain periodicity leading to crystalline structures. The dimer configuration of just two C60 buckyballs is described as an electrically insulating phase [17,18], while the solid fullerene C60 with linear chain alignment of the fullerene C60 molecules form a conducting phase [15]. Dimer formation does depend on pressure and temperature [16,19–22]. Burger et al. [19] investigated the phototransformation in C60 pristrin crystals. The C60 excitation was performed with 300 W of an Argon ion laser at 514.5 nm. 3-dimensional clusters of (C60)n with 60 atoms per molecule were formed at room temperature and slightly above. Dimers of 120 atomic C60-C60 appeared, if the excitation with 300 W Ar-laser were done at elevated temperatures around 360 to 380 K. Laranjeira et al. [22] describe that increasing pressure on the C60 dimers is able to change the distance between the two C60 covalently bonded molecules. For the 66/66 [2 + 2] cycloaddition dimer, they calculated a C60 molecular distance of 0.905 nm. They describe that very short distances, below 0.83 nm may alternatively be possible due to extreme pressure. Recently, it was shown that dense packed fullerene C60 needle-like crystals produced by aggregation-caused deposition from toluene-C60 solution have fluorescence properties [23]. Pressure-related aggregation-induced emission molecules are based on rearrangement of molecules [23,24].

In general, possibilities to stimulate fullerene C60 with different excitations are extraordinary interesting for actuator and sensor applications in numerous fields, like micromechanical systems or nanorobotics, semiconductor devices or biomedical structures. Sliding under certain normal forces like performed in the present study occurs in practical mechanical, micromechanical systems for electronic, biomedical uses and nano-energy collection often. In the present study, regulated minor sliding movements and frictional loading is applied on a ball-disc configuration. The impact on a fullerene C60 film produced by a simple vacuum-sublimation process is investigated to our knowledge for the first time with simple fluorescence microscopy visualization. Optical investigations were performed immediately and after 1 week.

In this experiment, the Revetest Scratch Tester RST3 (Anton Paar GmbH, Austria) was used to perform sliding friction tests. A Rockwell C indenter diamond tip (opening angle: 120°; radius: 0.2 mm) was applied to slide back and forth 10 times at a speed of 10 mm/min on polished commercially pure titanium sample or fullerene C₆₀ coated Ti disc.

Each sliding distance was 2 mm, resulting in a total sliding distance of 20 mm per scratch. All tests were conducted using the following parameters: Pre-scan and post-scan with a scanning load of 0.5 N and a sampling rate of 30.0 Hz. Sequential constant loads of (a) 0.5 N, (b) 1.0 N, (c) 2.0 N, (d) 3.0 N, and (e) 5.0 N (Fig. 1a) were applied on the same sample. Each scratch is 500 μm apart. The tribomechanical sliding tests on polished titanium samples (Fig. 1b) and on a fullerene C60 coated titanium sample (Fig. 1c) were performed in laboratory air with a relative humidity around 25 % without addition of intermediate medium. Such a sliding situation is named as “dry” in the present study. The wear volumes were calculated after determining cross-sectional areas of each sliding trace three times with digital profilometry (digital microscope KEYENCE 950-VHX, company Keyence GmbH, Deutschland). The mean value of the cross-sectional areas was multiplicated by the length of the sliding trace.

To avoid the influence of debris from continuous testing, the diamond tip was cleaned after each test. The testing process was recorded using software “Scratch 10.0.17” provided by Anton Paar GmbH. The coefficient of friction COF was calculated using the normal force Fn applied at each time point during the test and the detected frictional force Ft. Only the data from the outward passages will be used to calculate the COF. Eq. (1) shows the equation for calculation of the COF. Throughout the entire process, samples were handled with tweezers and stored in boxes or Petri dishes to prevent fluorescence contamination from direct contact with the scratches.
(1)
μ =
F t
F n
μ:coefficient of friction.
Ft: detected frictional force.
Fn: normal force.

Hitachi S-4800 scanning electron microscope (company Hitachi-Technologies Co., Japan) was utilized. Images were provided using an accelerating voltage of 2 keV and a current of 25 μA in secondary electron mode. The sample distance to the objective lens was 8.8 μm.

After completing the sliding tests, the sliding traces were immediately observed by using the KEYENCE BZ-X800 fluorescence microscope (company KEYENENCE GmbH, Deutschland). The KEYENCE BZ-X800 is a fully automated fluorescence imaging system that does not require a darkroom. Observations were made using three different filter sets with specific working wavelength ranges, as detailed in Table 2 .

All recorded images were taken using a 40 W fluorescent epi-illumination light source and a 4× objective lens. The exposure times for TRITC and GFP were 6 to 12 s, while the exposure time for DAPI was 1/8.5 s. The samples were observed and recorded again after one week. The surfaces of the samples in these experiments were observed by opening the filter cover and allowing daylight exposure. Daylight has a certain portion of ultraviolet UV radiation with wavelengths shorter than visible light. The fraction of UV light comprises about 10 % of the total electromagnetic radiation output from the sun. Throughout the entire process, tweezers were used to handle the samples, and they were stored in dedicated boxes or Petri dishes to ensure that the samples were not scratched or polluted by direct contact, preventing interference from fluorescent substances in subsequent operations. This careful handling method effectively protected the surface integrity of the samples, ensuring the accuracy of the experimental results.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Friction behavior of titanium without and with fullerene C60
ce:section.ce:para: The coefficient of friction of pure titanium under sliding motion of the diamond tip is 0.52 ± 0.06, if a normal load of 0.5 N was applied (Fig. 2a, Table 3 ). It minor decreases with increasing normal force. Finally, by applying a normal force of 5.0 N, the COF of cp-Ti is reduced to 0.42 ± 0.05, which makes a difference of 0.1.

For a normal force of 0.5 N, the C60 coating on titanium does considerably reduce the COF to an average value below 0.1, which is correlated to a superlow friction. The diamond tip did slide with sensational ease along the fullerene C60 film with an average COF of 0.05 ± 0.01, if the normal force was 0.5 N. However, this impressive behavior is occurring for the small normal force of 0.5 N only. For higher normal forces of 1.0 N to 3.0 N, the COF of Ti + C60 is around 0.4, which is a minor reduced COF in comparison to the cp-Ti without C60. (Fig. 2, Table 3).

Applying a normal force of 5.0 N, Ti with fullerene C60 film did show a reduced COF in comparison to cp-Ti without the fullerenes as well. COF is 0.42 ± 0.05 for cp-Ti. It is 0.39 ± 0.05 for Ti + C60, and is reduced slightly (Fig. 2e, Table 3) again. As mentioned before, in general, it can be observed that the fluctuations in COF curves are reduced, if the fullerene C60 film was on top of the titanium.

From Fig. 3 and Table 4 , it can be observed that the wear volume of titanium, whether or not it has a fullerene film on top, increases with the normal force set in the RST3. However, the increase in wear volume for titanium with a C60 coating Ti + C60 is smaller compared to pure titanium cp-Ti, especially under high load conditions (Fn = 5.0 N) or very low normal force (Fn = 0.5 N). Considering a normal force Fn of 3 N, the measurements showed that the average wear volume of Ti + C60 is slightly larger than that of cp-Ti. However, the calculated data for Ti + C60 have a larger standard deviation. In all other tests, the wear volume of Ti + C60 is significantly smaller than that of pure Ti, indicating that the addition of C60 significantly improves the wear resistance of titanium.

Fig. 4a shows the uncoated titanium surface after polishing with 1 μm diamond past. The hard diamond particles do leave polishing marks at the surface. After the high-vacuum sublimation process, the surface is covered with fullerene C60 film (Fig. 4b). It reveals a coral-like growth morphology. The fullerene C60 did grow as a film with porous branches. It appears to be a dendritic growth process. The sliding with 0.5 N (Fig. 5a) produced a smaller sliding trace in comparison to the sliding with 5.0 N (Fig. 5b). The sliding trace from loading with 0.5 N normal force shows a compressed fullerene film with remaining porous sections (Fig. 5c). A very special feature can be seen after sliding on top of the fullerene C60 film with 0.5 N normal force. There seem to be a lot of bright tiny points. This feature was not observed inside the other wear traces with 1.0 N, 2.0 N, 3.0 N or 5.0 N normal force sliding movements. By using a higher magnification and a longer exposure to the electron beam of the scanning electron microscope, the number of bright islands was growing (Fig. 5d). Not such bright tiny islands were seen inside the other sliding traces after applying 1.0 N, 2.0 N, 3.0 N or 5.0 N normal forces, but there are grooves and tiny metallic slabs inside these wear traces after the finish of the 10 passages of the diamond tip sliding (Fig. 5e,f). Between the extensively plastically deformed metal slabs, sometimes tiny pores appear. However, in comparison to the initially polished surface, the fraction of micropores is reduced. It seems to be smeared up due to plastic deformation of the titanium. At the edge of the sliding trace, the ductile metal seems to be spread above the coral-like fullerene C60 film due to the diamond sliding.

The images in Fig. 6 were taken using a fluorescence microscope KEYENCE BZ-X800 to capture the scratches on cp-Ti and Ti + C60 samples. Scratches from left to right correspond to sliding traces caused by normal forces Fn of 0.5 N, 1.0 N, 2.0 N, 3.0 N, and 5.0 N (Fig. 6b,e,h), respectively. It can be observed that after the tests with different normal forces, the fluorescence in the scratches on cp-Ti is not detectable (Fig. 6a,d,g). In contrast, in the Ti + C60 scratches, fluorescence is clearly visible under TRITC, GFP, and DAPI filter sets for all scratches except those caused by Fn = 0.5 N (Fig. 6b,e,h). After one week, the light emission capacity did disappear (Fig. 6c,f,i) again.

The results from friction measurements on cp-Ti and Ti with C60 under dry sliding conditions confirm different impacts and friction mechanisms of the fullerene C60 in dependence on the normal forces. The addition of an about 50 nm thick fullerene C60 film significantly reduces COF at medium to low normal forces (Fn ≤ 3 N) and stabilizes COF fluctuations under all normal forces applied in this study. For 5.0 N normal force, the COF values of Ti + C60 remained only slightly below the level of titanium without fullerene C60. This gives evidence that the sliding diamond tip is in contact with the titanium metal while 5.0 N were applied. The fullerene C60 topping is pushed away due to the impact of the diamond tip at such high normal forces like 5.0 N, and the diamond counterbody contacts the titanium substrate below as indicated by the COF values. Apparently, this is not the case for a normal force ten times lower: 0.5 N. At this loading regime, the maximum stresses do involve the fullerene film only. The diamond tip remains sliding against the fullerene film without touching the titanium substrate. This is confirmed by the exceedingly superlow COF of 0.05 ± 0.01 (Fig. 2, Table 3) as well as by scanning electron microscopy. Fullerene C60 film is densified and compressed after sliding with a normal force of 0.5 N (Fig. 5c). In addition, a multitude of loose agglomerates or particles are formed at the top of the compacted fullerene C60 film (Fig. 5d). These particles showed fast and strong charging effects in the SEM. Such a charging phenomenon under the impact of an electron beam in the SEM is typical for non-conductive substances. As stated before, the dimer C60-C60 structures of two covalently bonded buckyballs are an electrically insulating phase [17,18], Other solid fullerene C60 structures like linear chain alignment of the molecules form a conducting phase [16]. Therefore, it is possible that either during the sliding process with 0.5 N normal force or during the impact of the electron beam in the SEM, these detached particles may be able to form dimer C60-C60 structures.

If the normal forces are increased to 1.0 N, 2.0 N, 3.0 N or 5.0 N, the pressure on the fullerene film rises. It involves the titanium plastic deformation as can be seen from the drastic increase of the COF level in comparison to the superlow COF measured on titanium with fullerene C60 film at 0.5 N normal force. This observation is confirmed by SEM investigations as well (Fig. 5e,f). For 1.0 N normal force (Fig. 2b) and 2.0 N normal force (Fig. 2c), the COF curves of titanium with fullerene C60 still remained clearly below the titanium without fullerene films.

Not only, extremely reduced COF values of 0.05 ± 0.01 were achieved by a small normal load of 0.5 N, but fullerene C60 effectively reduces the wear volume. Low forces and moderate speed may maintain the fullerene C60 film very well. As discussed before, the diamond tip is not able to contact the titanium substrate, at normal force of 0.5 N. The sliding procedure was restricted to regions inside the fullerene C60 film (Fig. 7a). This observation is consolidated by the extremely low wear of titanium with fullerene C60 film. The wear volume is 9.2 ± 3.6 ·103 μm3 for Ti + C60, but eight times higher for the cp-Ti sample (Table 4).

Additionally, the irregular curvature of COF that was observed for polished titanium without C60 is obviously smoothed, if the fullerenes are present at the interface between the diamond tip and the titanium metallic surface. An explanation is given by the prevention of sticking and adherence due to the stiff and stable carbon buckyballs.

As stated before, the average wear volume of titanium with fullerene C60 film is 12 % of the wear volume of pure titanium without C60 film only, if a low normal force of 0.5 N was applied (Table 4). By using high normal forces like 5.0 N, the average wear volume was reduced due to the fullerene C60 film as well, but is around 80 % of the wear volume of commercially pure titanium without C60 film. This observation confirms different wear procedures as already suggested by the superlow COF below 0.1 which is not observed by applying higher normal forces like 5.0 N in the present study.

The extensive plastic deformation of the ductile commercially pure titanium under the effect of high normal forces is illustrated in Fig. 7b. The fullerene C60 film may be shifted and rearranged by the deformed metallic substrate under the strong pressure of the rigid diamond counterbody. The abrasive and adhesive wear mechanisms are pronounced, if the soft fullerene film is completely penetrated by the diamond tip. Tribo-oxidation may be favored due to the higher normal forces as well, because the tests operate under the impact of oxygen from ambient laboratory air.

Saha et al. [25] even completely opened the closed cages of C60 buckyball molecules by a controlled oxidation at 400 °C and 2 bar oxygen pressure. In this situation, a direct reaction of oxygen atoms with carbon atoms of fullerene C60 under the formation of CO and defective C60-x occurs. The direct removal of carbon atoms from the stable C60 fullerene structure due to local high energetic impact has been known for a long time [26]. Such open cages can provide highly activated sites for binding atoms and molecules from the environment. Gas adsorption was more than tripled due to direct removal of carbon atoms from single C60 molecules [25]. An appropriate friction during sliding movements like applied in the present study may be able to start opening of C60 cages. Friction could stimulate oxidation and removal of carbon atoms due to oxidation, but also adsorption of functional groups.

Huang et al. [6] modified fullerene C60 buckyballs by carboxylic groups. The C60-COOH shows excellent fluorescence. Tribo-oxidation can lead to the adsorption of oxygen and carbon oxides or humidity from the laboratory atmosphere. Light emitting properties of the fullerene C60 can be enhanced by reducing its symmetry [6,27,28]. Functional groups and attached radicals do change the fullerene C60 molecule symmetry.

At a normal force of 5.0 N, the wear volume of cp-Ti is strongly increased and amounts to 497.0 ± 10.0 ·103 μm3. The same is true for the Ti + C60. However, with a wear volume of 399.1 ± 64.6 ·103 μm3 (Table 4), the Ti + C60 material loss remains clearly below the cp-Ti at a normal force of 5.0 N. These results point to the fact that the fullerenes are still very effectively influencing the wear behavior, although the C60 film was penetrated by the diamond counterbody (Fig. 7b).

As illustrated in Fig. 3, the difference between the wear volume of cp-Ti and Ti + C60 is smaller for the normal forces of 1.0 N, 2.0 N and 3.0 N in comparison to 0.5 N or 5.0 N. It is obvious from the superlow COF that the diamond tip does slide in the fullerene C60 film during a low normal force of 0.5 N. Applying normal force of 5.0 N, there seemed to be a stimulation of an intermixing of the plastic deformable titanium with the buckyball molecules or their wear debris. It could lead to a certain reinforcement of the titanium in the loaded surface region. It is known that fullerene C60 is applied as a reinforcement to several metallic and organic materials for improving their mechanical strength and tribological resistance [29–32]. The intermixing effect can be expected to grow with increasing normal force. It may be especially effective for 5.0 N in the present study. It is not at all occurring at a normal force of 0.5 N. Chen et al. [29] report a refined microstructure after the addition of 0.2 wt% C60 to Sn-Ag-Cu-alloy. In their studies, the refined microstructure gave rise to a 12.1 % and 19.9 % improvement in shear strength and microhardness.

Fullerene C60 is named by He et al. [33] as a kind of electronegative molecule. They describe that fullerene C60 can be beneficial for fast photo-generated electron transfer since the majority of its π orbital are mostly outside the cage. Besides, the electronic structure of C60 is instinctively concentrated to the surface of 30 π-orbitals [34,35]. C60 in fcc structure is a semiconducting material with a band gap of 1.7 eV [36,37].

As stated before, inducing excited states of buckminsterfullerene C60 can be performed by different methods [38,39]. Tukhbatullin et al. [38] describe that the addition of C60 and C70 fullerene crystals to crystalline terbium sulphate leads to strong quenching of the photoluminescence of Tb3+ ion, which takes place by energy transfer to the fullerene molecules in this case. Tukhbatullin et al. argue that the small size and high degree of surface curvature of the fullerene molecules, as well as the electrical properties of their crystals contribute to the formation of strong local electric fields, resulting in stable electron emission. There is a high electron mobility and charge carrier concentration possible. Bulgakov et al. [39] report about the quenching of excited states of the fullerene C60 and C70. The dipole moments of the C60 and C70 fullerenes in the unexcited (singlet) state are equal to zero due to their high molecular symmetry. However, the π electrons systems are easily polarized in both carbon molecules [39]. They argue that for this reason, the dipole moments of the fullerenes do develop in the interaction with the electron donor systems. Bulgakov et al. found that the polarizability for the C70 and C60 molecules is equal to 10.27 nm3 and 8.27 nm3, respectively.

Also, the composition of wear debris from the sliding process deserves closer considerations. As aforementioned, tribo-oxidation could lead to open cages of fullerene C60-x. Kumar et al. [40] describe a photoinduced electron transfer from graphene to fullerene for potential use in development of photoresponsive nanostructures. The high electron acceptor fullerene and the high charge transport material graphene or oxidised graphene should be exploited in the approach of Kumar et al.. The formation of graphene, oxidised graphene oxide, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and oxidative debris (OD) during the wear processes due to sliding loading of fullerene cages is conceivable. The triboelectric charged strong electron acceptor fullerene could provide electric charging to nanoscale light emitters like CQDs and ODs [41–44], if such nanostructures are produced during sliding processes. As aforementioned, tribo-oxidation may cause C60 cage opening and generation of C60-x due to oxidative removal of carbon atoms from the buckyball molecules. Lu et al. [45] report about transforming C60 cages into graphene quantum dots (GQDs). Their explanation of the transforming mechanism is based on temperature increase and catalytic effects due to intermixing of fullerene C60 with ruthenium single crystals. Lu et al. describe that the C60 molecules are embedded in the surface of Ru crystals and are gradually fractured into carbon clusters. GQDs have excellent photostability and photoluminescence properties. They are reflective in the ultraviolet region [44,46,47].

The already described unique structural features of the buckyball molecule C60 (the symmetry, the conjugate large π bond system etc.) is able to provide rapid transfer of photo-excited electrons, but slow recombination of electrons and holes [33]. According to the observations in the present studies, frictional movements seem to favor the light emission capabilities of fullerene C60 conjugated material systems like open-cage C60 or fractured residuals from broken fullerenes. Friction is known to produce heat and electric charge effects. Due to such friction, non or less conductive materials experience electric charging. Considering the buckminsterfullerene C60, it can arrange in different 3dimensional crystalline structures or form 2dimensional polymeric chains. As aforementioned, C60-C60 dimers belong to non-conducting inorganic molecules. Also, the presence of isolating dimers would promote tribo-electric charging effects in the tribological contact zone during sliding. It was suggested that theoretically one C60 molecule can accept up to six electrons [48–50]. The buckminsterfullerene C60 obtains a threefold degenerate lowest unoccupied molecular orbital. It holds electrons in the delocalized pseudo-π orbital [51]. Hu et al. [52] demonstrated that there is an increase in tribo-electric charges with normal load due to the enhanced normal area. This finding can be very well correlated to the stronger light emission at higher normal forces as indicated in Fig. 6b,i,h.

Additionally, tribocharging of titania sites at the top of the metallic titanium can be expected due to the friction under different normal forces at surface asperities of the tribological contact zone [52,53]. Mechanisms of titania nanoparticles and nanofilms capable of collection and storage of electrons are suggested [54–58]. Metal ion sites at surface vacancies have been proposed to act as traps for electrons [56]. Usually, the TiO2 film on top of the metal titanium has a thickness of around 5 nm [59], if there is no additional friction in oxygen-containing atmosphere like laboratory air. Such a titania film thickness would be in the range of the size of defective nanoparticles or nanofilms for electron storage. However, it can be expected that there is an additional impact of pressure and temperature increase due to the friction under the sliding impact of the diamond counter body. Also, this idea is further promoted because with increasing normal load from 0.5 N to 5.0 N, the intensity of light emission from the Ti + C60 scratches do increase clearly (Fig. 6b,I,h).

Furthermore, one needs to consider the impact of fullerene C60 film thickness. The fullerenes were deposited by high-vacuum sublimation. The film thickness was around 50 nm before the tribological loading. However, if there is a transfer of electrons from the semiconducting electron collector TiO2 to the fullerene molecules, it will happen directly at the TiO2-C60 interface only. As discussed before several times, in the present study, it was shown that an increase of normal force causes a more intense emission of light with the most intense photon excitation after sliding with 5.0 N normal force. Pressing and moving the fullerene buckyballs closely and intensively onto the surface asperities of TiO2 covered titanium sample is favored with increasing normal force. Hereby, a strong tribo-induced charge transfer of electrons between the semiconductor nano-sized TiO2 and the C60 could occur (Fig. 8 ).

Although some research groups present the formation of instant charges even for fullerites without any mechanical impacts [60–62], the tribomechanical stimulation by simple sliding would provide a novel method to sense the intensity of relative motion and friction by effected light emission signals.

The tribological induced light emission capability was observed in red, green and blue color wave ranges. In any case, for this light signaling ability an excitation with daylight was necessary which contains a certain fraction of UV radiation. This may be caused by the fullerene C60 maximum absorption in the ultraviolet region of light between 190 nm and 410 nm and a weaker absorption band extending to 700 nm [35,58,63–66]. Also, cage-opened fullerenes or their fractured residuals and other fullerene wear debris, especially CQNs and GQDs can emit various colors of light due to UV light radiation [44–46].

Strong chemical agents like sulfuric acid or sodium nitrate [67,68] are required for the oxidative transformation of fullerene C60 in example into graphene quantum dots. If the tribomechanical loading with the chosen parameters of the present study was sufficient to produce CQDs and GQDs or just open-cage C60-x, or, if it favored the adsorption of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on fullerene C60 for producing oxidised oxC60 [68] need to be proven in future research. The same is true for proving the influence of pressure on promotion TiO2 formation and carbon-TiO2 contact or carbon‑titanium intermixing for quenching effects of the photoluminescence properties.

Fullerene C60 can be stimulated and/or transformed by tribomechanical loading. Light emission was observed after sliding with different normal forces and daylight exposure on Ti + C60. It indicates that fullerenes were raised into an excited state or do form excitable residuals or wear debris. Such functional friction promoted excited states of nano‑carbons and correlative optical signaling may be of importance for several applications in biomedical field or surface engineering in general. Activation and charging effects are typical phenomena during friction. Electrical bad and non-conducting materials like the electrical non-conductive diamond tip, the semiconducting fullerene C60 or even isolating C60 −C60 dimers are involved. The molecule C60 as a strong electron acceptor is able to host additional electrons from friction processes. So, excited states of the fullerene C60 but as well the ability to collect additional electrons may be influencing the strong photoluminescence.

In the present study, the light emission capability was observed for several hours. Hereby, the C60 molecules or their residuals and wear debris are considerably more stimulated in comparison to the non tribomechanical loaded remaining C60 film. During the later excitation by daylight, the strong contact between tribomechanical stimulated fullerene C60 or it's residuals and the TiO2 on top of the titanium metal could further promote photoluminescent properties. If, photoinduced charge transfer between nanoscaled TiO2 and electrical nano-collectors like C60 molecules or wear debris worked, nanoscaled discharge retarders that can be excited or stimulated for a while by irradiation. Excited states of fullerene C60, open-cage C60-x, oxC60 or wear debris with different functional groups including CQDs or GQDs obtain different mechanisms of luminescence, like intrinsic or extrinsic types. They may be able to emit with different light intensities.

While light emission increased with the higher normal forces, the measurement of friction and the wear volume point to two different wear mechanisms. For a low normal force of 0.5 N, the COF of C60 coated titanium remained ultralow with a value below 0.1. At a normal load of 0.5 N, no plastic deformation of the metallic titanium is included. The diamond tip interacted with the fullerene C60 coating and shifted the molecules which apparently cause a low sliding resistance due to minor intermolecular interaction. Higher normal loads of 1.0 N and above do include the plastic deformation of the titanium substrate. In this way, an intermixing of the fullerene C60 molecules and the titanium metal can be expected. A reduced wear was observed in the present study. It could be caused by certain reinforcing effects or even sliding effects due to carbonization. Open-cage fullerene residuals as well as fractured fullerene wear debris like defective tribo-oxidised graphene flakes, CQDs or GQDs could favor both phenomena, the reinforcement of the titanium metal as well as light emission under UV radiation.

Chengtian Chen: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Annett Dorner-Reisel: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization. Tao Wang: Methodology, Funding acquisition. Emma Freiberger: Methodology, Investigation. Doreen Schneider: Investigation. Uwe Ritter: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Methodology, Investigation, Funding acquisition. Jens Moje: Writing – review & editing, Resources, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support by German Ministry for Research and Technology (Bundesministerium für Bildung und Forschung, BMBF, grant no. 13GW0401). The authors are thankful for the support by the International Science and Technology Cooperation Programme (2018YFE0194100). Sincere thanks are given to Mr. Hartwig Albrecht (Schmalkalden University of Applied Sciences, Germany) for technical support with the Revetest Scratch Tester RST3 equipment.
